title: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
body_html:
Background
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a rare but life-threatening form of thrombotic microangiopathy characterised by microangiopathic hemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and end-organ damage. TTP represents a medical emergency with untreated mortality approaching 90%, though this can be dramatically reduced to 10-20% with prompt treatment.

Epidemiology
TTP is a rare condition with an estimated incidence of 6 per 1,000,000 per year in the UK, though recognition is increasing. The disease predominantly affects adults with peak incidence in the fourth decade of life, showing a female predominance with a 3:2 female-to-male ratio. While very rare in children, when it does occur, approximately 35% present with neurological symptoms such as hemiparesis, hemiplegia, or seizure.

Pathophysiology
TTP results from a deficiency of ADAMTS13 (a disintegrin and metalloproteinase), a plasma von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease. This deficiency leads to the accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers, which have tremendous avidity for binding platelets and initiating spontaneous thrombus formation.

The condition can be:
  • Congenital: Due to mutations in the ADAMTS13 gene
  • Acquired: More common, caused by autoantibodies targeting ADAMTS13

ADAMTS13 activity levels below 10% are diagnostic for TTP. The resulting microthrombi formation leads to end-organ ischaemia and damage, with the central nervous system and kidneys being the most commonly affected organs.

Clinical presentation
TTP was historically characterised by a classic pentad of fever, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anaemia, renal dysfunction, and neurological dysfunction. However, this complete pentad is present in fewer than 5% of cases. Modern understanding recognises that patients can present with varying combinations of these features.

Common presenting features:
  • Gastrointestinal symptoms: 69%
  • Weakness: 63%
  • Bleeding or purpura: 54%
  • Major neurological findings (coma, stroke, seizure, focal abnormalities): 41%
  • Minor neurological findings (headache, confusion): 26%
  • Fever and chills: 10%

Physical examination findings:
  • Fever
  • Purpura and petechiae (non-palpable, associated with platelet count <50 × 10⁹/L)
  • Jaundice (secondary to haemolysis)
  • Severe hypertension
  • Neurological abnormalities
  • Splenomegaly

Investigations and diagnosis

Laboratory Tests:
  • Blood smear: Presence of schistocytes (fragmented erythrocytes) - hallmark finding
  • Complete blood count: Anaemia and thrombocytopenia
  • Haemolysis markers: Elevated LDH (extremely high), elevated indirect bilirubin, elevated reticulocyte count, decreased haptoglobin
  • Renal function: Creatinine mildly elevated in about half of patients
  • Urinalysis: Proteinuria and microscopic haematuria
  • ADAMTS13 activity and antibody levels: Activity <10% confirms diagnosis

PLASMIC Score:
A validated screening tool to predict likelihood of ADAMTS13 deficiency:
- Platelet count <30,000/μL
- Haemolysis present
- MCV <90 fL
- INR <1.5
- Creatinine <2.0 mg/dL
- No active cancer
- No organ transplantation

Scores >5 suggest high probability of TTP (sensitivity 99%, specificity 57%).

Additional Tests:
  • HIV, hepatitis B and C serology
  • Autoantibody screen
  • Pregnancy test (when appropriate)
  • Cardiac troponin (elevated levels indicate poor prognosis)

Management
TTP is a haematological emergency requiring immediate intervention, ideally within 4-8 hours of presentation.

First-Line Treatments:
Plasma Exchange (Plasmapheresis): Gold standard treatment
  • Removes inhibitory antibodies and replaces deficient ADAMTS13
  • Daily treatments of one plasma volume (40 mL/kg)
  • Continued until platelet count >150,000/μL for >48 hours
  • If plasma exchange unavailable include fresh frozen plasma infusion may be used

Corticosteroids:
  • Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for neurologically intact patients
  • IV methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 3 days or 125 mg 2-4 times daily for severely ill patients
  • Continue throughout plasma exchange course

Adjunctive and Second-Line Treatments:
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody
  • Recommended for refractory/relapsing cases
  • Consider early in acute cases with neurological/cardiac involvement
  • May prevent relapses

Caplacizumab: Novel humanised monoclonal antibody fragment
  • Blocks platelet adhesion to von Willebrand factor
  • Rapid onset, significantly reduces mortality
  • Given IV initially, then subcutaneous for 30 days post-plasma exchange

Supportive care:
  • Red cell transfusion as clinically indicated
  • Folate supplementation during active hemolysis
  • Thromboprophylaxis with LMWH when platelets >50 × 10⁹/L
  • Contraindication: Platelet transfusion (risk of thrombotic events unless life-threatening haemorrhage)

Prognosis:
  • Untreated mortality: 90%
  • Treated mortality: 10-20%
  • Approximately one-third of survivors experience relapse within 10 years
  • Early treatment significantly improves outcomes

Complications:
  • End-organ damage (neurological, renal, cardiac)
  • Complications of plasma exchange (central line infections, TRALI)
  • Bleeding complications with anticoagulation
  • Long-term neurological sequelae

Special Considerations

Pregnancy:
  • Accounts for 10-25% of TTP cases
  • Course not altered by pregnancy termination
  • Requires specialised management

Drug-Induced TTP:
  • Clopidogrel-associated TTP occurs in 1:20,000 patients
  • Usually within first two weeks of therapy
  • 75% have normal ADAMTS13 levels
  • Requires immediate drug cessation plus plasma exchange

Monitoring and Follow-up:
  • Daily hemolysis markers during treatment
  • ADAMTS13 activity monitoring
  • Watch for relapse signs and symptoms
  • Long-term follow-up for potential complications
